question.text: Rajesh holds 600 shares of ABC Ltd. with a face value of Rs.10 each. If ABC Ltd. announces a stock split to reduce the face value to Rs.2, how many shares will Rajesh hold after the split?

explanation.text: **The Logic:** In a stock split, total face value held remains constant. Rajesh holds 600 × Rs.10 = Rs.6,000 in total face value. After split to FV Rs.2: 6,000 / 2 = 3,000 shares. The split ratio is 10:2 or 5:1, meaning each old share becomes 5 new shares (600 × 5 = 3,000). **The Trap:** Some candidates may divide instead of multiply (getting 120). Remember: when face value decreases, number of shares increases proportionally. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series VIII covers derivative contract adjustments after stock splits (lot size adjustment); NISM Series XII covers basic stock split mechanics. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** After a stock split, each share will trade at approximately 1/5th of the pre-split price (in this example). Reassure clients that their total investment value hasn't changed -- they just hold more shares at a lower per-share price.
